# Total Annual Electricity Cost for a 200 MW Data Center in Houston, TX (2025)

## Executive Summary

| Component | Annual Cost |
|---|---|
| **Energy Cost** (Day-Ahead Houston Hub LMPs) | **$48,614,451** |
| **Transmission Cost** (4CP-based) | **$13,151,720** |
| **TOTAL ANNUAL ELECTRICITY COST** | **≈ $61,766,171** |
| Total Energy Consumption | 1,384,334 MWh |
| Effective All-In Cost | ~$44.62/MWh |

---

## Part 1: Energy Cost — $48,614,451

### Methodology
I retrieved all 8,760 hourly day-ahead Houston Hub LMPs for 2025 from the ERCOT database (`ercot_db.ercot_lmp` table, `zone_name = 'houston'`, `zone_type = 'hub'`, `is_day_ahead = true`). For each month, I computed the average price for each hour-of-day, then applied the seasonal load profiles to the 200 MW capacity.

### Seasonal Mapping
- **Winter** (Dec, Jan, Feb): Winter profile
- **Spring** (Mar, Apr, May): Spring profile
- **Summer** (Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep): Summer profile
- **Fall** (Oct, Nov): Fall profile

### Monthly Breakdown

| Month | Season | Energy (MWh) | Energy Cost ($) | Avg Price ($/MWh) |
|-------|--------|-------------|----------------|-------------------|
| Jan | Winter | 118,544 | 3,460,546 | 29.19 |
| Feb | Winter | 107,072 | 3,332,667 | 31.13 |
| Mar | Spring | 117,366 | 4,009,662 | 34.16 |
| Apr | Spring | 113,580 | 4,193,615 | 36.92 |
| May | Spring | 117,366 | 4,909,319 | 41.83 |
| Jun | Summer | 113,340 | 4,085,707 | 36.05 |
| Jul | Summer | 117,118 | 4,464,319 | 38.12 |
| Aug | Summer | 117,118 | 4,829,809 | 41.24 |
| Sep | Summer | 113,340 | 3,654,162 | 32.24 |
| Oct | Fall | 117,366 | 4,037,897 | 34.40 |
| Nov | Fall | 113,580 | 4,082,604 | 35.94 |
| Dec | Winter | 118,544 | 3,554,145 | 29.98 |
| **Total** | | **1,384,334** | **$48,614,451** | **$35.12** |

**Source:** ERCOT day-ahead Houston Hub LMP data from the `ercot_db` database (8,760 hourly records for Jan 1 – Dec 31, 2025).

---

## Part 2: Transmission Cost — $13,151,720

### Applicable Transmission Rate
The ERCOT-wide wholesale transmission rate for 2025 is **$66.76/kW-year** ($66,760/MW-year), as established in **PUCT Docket No. 57491** (Commission Staff's Final Transmission Charge Matrix, March 20, 2025). This rate represents the total ERCOT Transmission Cost of Service (TCOS) revenue requirement of ~$5.4 billion divided by the ERCOT-wide 4CP demand.

**Source:** NRG Energy remarks by Travis Kavulla (Vice President, Regulatory Affairs) at the ERCOT Market Summit, Austin, TX, February 25, 2025 — *"ERCOT's transmission rate is currently $66.76 per kilowatt-year."* ([NRG Energy PDF](https://www.nrg.com/assets/documents/energy-policy/ercot-transmission-costs-and-rate-design-remarks-on-feb-25-2025.pdf))

### 4CP Calculation

Under ERCOT's 4CP rules (16 TAC § 25.192), transmission charges for large transmission-connected customers are based on their average demand during the four ERCOT coincident peak intervals — one each in June, July, August, and September.

The **official 2025 4CP intervals** (published by ERCOT on December 1, 2025) are:

| Month | 4CP Interval | ERCOT System Peak |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| June | 6/19/2025 17:00 | 77.49 GW |
| July | 7/30/2025 17:00 | 81.91 GW |
| August | 8/18/2025 17:00 | 83.97 GW |
| September | 9/04/2025 17:30 | 80.08 GW |

**Source:** [Energy Edge Consulting — "ERCOT Official 2025 4CP Intervals Released"](https://energyedge.com/ercot-official-2025-4cp-intervals-released/)

### Data Center Load at 4CP Intervals

All four 4CP intervals occurred in the late afternoon (17:00–17:30), which maps to the summer profile at hours 16–17 (0-indexed):

| 4CP Interval | Summer Profile Factor | Load (MW) |
|---|---|---|
| June (HE 17) | 0.99 | 198.0 |
| July (HE 17) | 0.99 | 198.0 |
| August (HE 17) | 0.99 | 198.0 |
| September (HE 17.5) | 0.97 | 194.0 |
| **Average 4CP Load** | | **197.0 MW** |

### Transmission Charge Calculation

$$\text{Annual Transmission Charge} = \text{Avg 4CP Load} \times \text{Transmission Rate}$$
$$= 197.0 \text{ MW} \times \$66{,}760/\text{MW-year}$$
$$= \$13{,}151{,}720$$

---

## Part 3: Total Annual Electricity Cost

| Cost Component | Amount |
|---|---|
| Energy Cost (Day-Ahead Houston Hub) | $48,614,451 |
| Transmission Cost (4CP-based, $66.76/kW-yr) | $13,151,720 |
| **TOTAL** | **≈ $61,766,171** |

### Key Metrics
- **Total annual energy consumption:** 1,384,334 MWh (~79.2% capacity factor)
- **Average 4CP demand:** 197.0 MW (98.5% of nameplate)
- **Average energy price:** $35.12/MWh
- **Effective all-in cost:** ~$44.62/MWh

### Assumptions
1. The data center is exposed to day-ahead ERCOT Houston Hub prices for all energy consumption (no bilateral PPAs or real-time exposure).
2. The ERCOT-wide transmission rate of $66.76/kW-year applies as the applicable transmission rate for 2025 (per PUCT Docket 57491). This is the system-wide rate; individual TSP rates (e.g., CenterPoint Houston's specific TCOS rate) may differ slightly, but the $66.76/kW-year is the commonly cited ERCOT-wide figure used for large load cost analysis.
3. The data center does not curtail during 4CP intervals (runs at full profile throughout).
4. Only energy and transmission-related charges are included — no distribution charges, ancillary service charges, metering fees, or taxes are considered, per the question's scope.
5. The seasonal profiles apply uniformly across all days within each season, and the average monthly-hourly day-ahead prices represent the expected energy costs.

(NRG) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments in this project in response to the Staff’s questions regarding the directive in Senate Bill 6 (SB6) 1 for the Public Utility Commission of Texas (Commission or PUC) to evaluate various aspects of transmission cost recovery and allocation, including the existing four coincident peak (4CP) methodology. NRG is the parent company of well-established retail electric providers (REPs) and power generation companies (PGCs) in the competitive electricity markets in Texas.\n\nI. INTRODUCTION\n\nNRG appreciates the Commission’s thorough evaluation of the existing 4CP transmission cost allocation mechanism. NRG first formally raised concerns with the 4CP mechanism in the spring of 2017 as outlined in the report titled “Priorities for the Evolution of an Energy-Only Electricity Market Design in ERCOT” 2by Dr. William Hogan and Dr. Susan Pope. On a net basis, there are no cost savings, only a reallocation of the costs to other customers. 3\n\nThe 4CP mechanism is not just outdated, it has become an increasingly unreasonable method of allocating transmission costs because it is completely disconnected from the actual costs incurred to develop, maintain, and operate the ERCOT transmission system. Its protracted existence has resulted in significant transmission cost shifting benefitting some customer classes\n\n1 89 th Tex. The three steps of the 4CP method of allocating transmission costs are discussed in detail in Section II.A of these comments but can be briefly summarized as follows:\n\n• Step 1, Allocation to DSPs: ERCOT-wide transmission costs are allocated to \ndistribution service providers (DSPs) (i.e., ERCOT transmission and distribution \nutilities, municipally-owned utilities, and electric cooperatives) based on 4CP. \n• Step 2, Allocation to Rate Classes: DSPs allocate their share of transmission costs \nto their rate classes (e.g., Residential, Secondary ≤ 10kW, Transmission). DSPs for \nthe Investor-Owned Utilities (IOUs) allocate transmission costs to their rate classes \nbased on 4CP. \n• Step 3, Recovery from Customers: DSPs recover transmission costs from \ncustomers in each rate class based on billing determinants that differ based on rate \nclasses (e.g., Residential customers are billed on a volumetric (per kilowatt-hour \n(kWh)) basis; Transmission customers are billed on 4CP). 4 \n\nThe first two steps of this process involve cost allocation while the third step involves rate design. Regarding scope, NRG offers that it would be most effective for the Commission’s focus in this proceeding to be on Steps 1 and 2 – changing the methodology to allocate transmission costs to all DSPs (including municipally-owned utilities and electric cooperatives) and among consumer classes for each DSP in the IOU service territories. The complexities of rate design (Step 3) are arguably best left to the side until the allocation issues attendant to Steps 1 and 2 have been fully re-examined.\n\n4In areas of the state open to retail competition, DSPs invoice retail electric providers (REPs) to recover their costs. REPs are the primary point of contact for end-use customers and are responsible for paying DSPs for all transmission and distribution charges applicable to customers the REP serves.\n\nAs more fully detailed below, NRG maintains that the allocation of transmission costs to DSPs (Step 1) and the allocation of costs from the IOU DSPs to their customer classes (Step 2) 5 should be changed from the current 4CP mechanism to something more equitable, so that the costs that are ultimately allocated to individual customers are more fairly split from the outset. NRG also suggests that should the Commission wish to pursue changes to the structure of transmission rates for the customer classes of DSPs within the Commission’s ratemaking jurisdiction, one way to further accommodate the impacts of large loads, besides moving away from 4CP which should happen regardless, would be to create a new rate structure (e.g., a subclass) for loads greater than 75 MW within the transmission class for IOU territories for those customers.\n\nII. IN RESPONSE TO STAFF’S AUGUST 5, 2025 QUESTIONS\n\nNRG Energy, Inc. (NRG) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments in this project in response to the Staff’s questions regarding the directive in Senate Bill 6 (SB6) 1 for the Public Utility Commission of Texas (Commission or PUC) to evaluate various aspects of transmission cost recovery and allocation, including the existing four coincident peak (4CP) methodology. NRG is the parent company of well-established retail electric providers (REPs) and power generation companies (PGCs) in the competitive electricity markets in Texas.\n\nI. INTRODUCTION\n\nNRG appreciates the Commission’s thorough evaluation of the existing 4CP transmission cost allocation mechanism. NRG first formally raised concerns with the 4CP mechanism in the spring of 2017 as outlined in the report titled “Priorities for the Evolution of an Energy-Only Electricity Market Design in ERCOT” 2by Dr. William Hogan and Dr. Susan Pope. In their analysis and extensive critique of 4CP, they concluded:\n\nDemand reductions resulting from the 4CP transmission cost‐recovery mechanism are not in response to high system marginal costs, but instead are in response to the allocation of sunk costs. On a net basis, there are no cost savings, only a reallocation of the costs to other customers. 3\n\nThe 4CP mechanism is not just outdated, it has become an increasingly unreasonable method of allocating transmission costs because it is completely disconnected from the actual costs incurred to develop, maintain, and operate the ERCOT transmission system. Its protracted existence has resulted in significant transmission cost shifting benefitting some customer classes\n\n1 89 th Tex. Leg., R.S., Senate Bill 6, § 6 (effective June 20, 2025). \n2 See Project to Assess Price Formation Rules in ERCOT’s Energy-Only Market, Project No. 47199, William \nW. Hogan & Susan L. NRG and Calpine \nCorporation supported the development of this paper. \n3Id. at 78 (Bates page 83). \n\nNRG COMMENTS ON STAFF QUESTIONS 2 to the detriment of others. Therefore, this effort to update the transmission cost allocation mechanism so that it more equitably distributes the cost burden among customer classes is critically important, and NRG looks forward to participating in all aspects of the effort.\n\nNRG offers that a meaningful analysis of the 4CP methodology should begin with a shared understanding of the mechanics by which transmission costs are allocated today (the “steps”) and agreement on which steps of that process will be evaluated in this proceeding (the “scope”). The three steps of the 4CP method of allocating transmission costs are discussed in detail in Section II.A of these comments but can be briefly summarized as follows:\n\n• Step 1, Allocation to DSPs: ERCOT-wide transmission costs are allocated to \ndistribution service providers (DSPs) (i.e., ERCOT transmission and distribution \nutilities, municipally-owned utilities, and electric cooperatives) based on 4CP. \n• Step 2, Allocation to Rate Classes: DSPs allocate their share of transmission costs \nto their rate classes (e.g., Residential, Secondary ≤ 10kW, Transmission). DSPs for \nthe Investor-Owned Utilities (IOUs) allocate transmission costs to their rate classes \nbased on 4CP. \n• Step 3, Recovery from Customers: DSPs recover transmission costs from \ncustomers in each rate class based on billing determinants that differ based on rate \nclasses (e.g., Residential customers are billed on a volumetric (per kilowatt-hour \n(kWh)) basis; Transmission customers are billed on 4CP). 4 \n\nThe first two steps of this process involve cost allocation while the third step involves rate design. Regarding scope, NRG offers that it would be most effective for the Commission’s focus in this proceeding to be on Steps 1 and 2 – changing the methodology to allocate transmission costs to all DSPs (including municipally-owned utilities and electric cooperatives) and among consumer classes for each DSP in the IOU service territories. The complexities of rate design (Step 3) are arguably best left to the side until the allocation issues attendant to Steps 1 and 2 have been fully re-examined.\n\n4In areas of the state open to retail competition, DSPs invoice retail electric providers (REPs) to recover their costs. REPs are the primary point of contact for end-use customers and are responsible for paying DSPs for all transmission and distribution charges applicable to customers the REP serves.\n\nAs more fully detailed below, NRG maintains that the allocation of transmission costs to DSPs (Step 1) and the allocation of costs from the IOU DSPs to their customer classes (Step 2) 5 should be changed from the current 4CP mechanism to something more equitable, so that the costs that are ultimately allocated to individual customers are more fairly split from the outset. NRG also suggests that should the Commission wish to pursue changes to the structure of transmission rates for the customer classes of DSPs within the Commission’s ratemaking jurisdiction, one way to further accommodate the impacts of large loads, besides moving away from 4CP which should happen regardless, would be to create a new rate structure (e.g., a subclass) for loads greater than 75 MW within the transmission class for IOU territories for those customers.\n\nII. BACKGROUND A. tool_input.query:
SELECT 
  EXTRACT(MONTH FROM timestamp) as month,
  COUNT(*) as hours,
  ROUND(AVG(lmp)::numeric, 2) as avg_price,
  ROUND(SUM(lmp)::numeric, 2) as sum_price,
  ROUND(MIN(lmp)::numeric, 2) as min_price,
  ROUND(MAX(lmp)::numeric, 2) as max_price
FROM ercot_lmp 
WHERE zone_name = 'houston' 
AND zone_type = 'hub'
AND is_day_ahead = true 
AND timestamp >= '2025-01-01' 
AND timestamp < '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY EXTRACT(MONTH FROM timestamp)
ORDER BY month

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
  EXTRACT(MONTH FROM timestamp)::int as month,
  EXTRACT(HOUR FROM timestamp)::int as hour,
  ROUND(AVG(lmp)::numeric, 4) as avg_price,
  ROUND(SUM(lmp)::numeric, 4) as sum_price,
  COUNT(*) as cnt
FROM ercot_lmp 
WHERE zone_name = 'houston' 
AND zone_type = 'hub'
AND is_day_ahead = true 
AND timestamp >= '2025-01-01' 
AND timestamp < '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY EXTRACT(MONTH FROM timestamp), EXTRACT(HOUR FROM timestamp)
ORDER BY month, hour

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load the monthly-hourly average prices
df = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260616_182208.csv')

# Create a pivot table: month x hour -> avg_price
price_matrix = df.pivot(index='month', columns='hour', values='avg_price')

# Seasonal load profiles (24 hours each)
# Winter: Dec, Jan, Feb (months 12, 1, 2)
# Spring: Mar, Apr, May (months 3, 4, 5)
# Summer: Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep (months 6, 7, 8, 9)
# Fall: Oct, Nov (months 10, 11)

winter_profile = [0.62, 0.60, 0.58, 0.57, 0.58, 0.62, 0.68, 0.74, 0.81, 0.88, 0.94, 0.98, 1.00, 1.00, 0.99, 0.97, 0.95, 0.93, 0.90, 0.86, 0.81, 0.75, 0.70, 0.66]
spring_profile = [0.58, 0.56, 0.54, 0.53, 0.54, 0.58, 0.64, 0.71, 0.79, 0.87, 0.94, 0.99, 1.00, 1.00, 1.00, 0.99, 0.97, 0.95, 0.92, 0.88, 0.83, 0.77, 0.71, 0.64]
summer_profile = [0.55, 0.53, 0.51, 0.50, 0.51, 0.55, 0.61, 0.69, 0.78, 0.88, 0.96, 1.00, 1.00, 1.00, 1.00, 1.00, 0.99, 0.97, 0.95, 0.91, 0.86, 0.80, 0.73, 0.61]
fall_profile   = [0.58, 0.56, 0.54, 0.53, 0.54, 0.58, 0.64, 0.71, 0.79, 0.87, 0.94, 0.99, 1.00, 1.00, 1.00, 0.99, 0.97, 0.95, 0.92, 0.88, 0.83, 0.77, 0.71, 0.64]

# Map months to seasons
season_map = {
    1: 'winter', 2: 'winter', 12: 'winter',
    3: 'spring', 4: 'spring', 5: 'spring',
    6: 'summer', 7: 'summer', 8: 'summer', 9: 'summer',
    10: 'fall', 11: 'fall'
}

season_profiles = {
    'winter': winter_profile,
    'spring': spring_profile,
    'summer': summer_profile,
    'fall': fall_profile
}

# Days in each month for 2025 (non-leap year)
days_in_month = {
    1: 31, 2: 28, 3: 31, 4: 30, 5: 31, 6: 30,
    7: 31, 8: 31, 9: 30, 10: 31, 11: 30, 12: 31
}

# Data center capacity
capacity_mw = 200

# ============================================================
# PART 1: ENERGY COST
# ============================================================
# For each month, for each hour, energy cost = avg_price * profile[hour] * capacity_mw * days_in_month
# The avg_price is the average day-ahead price for that month-hour combination
# The profile gives the fraction of capacity at each hour
# Energy (MWh) = profile[hour] * capacity_mw * 1 hour * days_in_month
# Cost = Energy * avg_price

total_energy_cost = 0
total_energy_mwh = 0
monthly_breakdown = {}

for month in range(1, 13):
    season = season_map[month]
    profile = season_profiles[season]
    days = days_in_month[month]
    
    month_energy_cost = 0
    month_energy_mwh = 0
    
    for hour in range(24):
        avg_price = price_matrix.loc[month, hour]
        load_fraction = profile[hour]
        load_mw = load_fraction * capacity_mw
        energy_mwh = load_mw * 1 * days  # MWh for the month at this hour
        cost = energy_mwh * avg_price
        month_energy_cost += cost
        month_energy_mwh += energy_mwh
    
    total_energy_cost += month_energy_cost
    total_energy_mwh += month_energy_mwh
    monthly_breakdown[month] = {
        'season': season,
        'energy_mwh': month_energy_mwh,
        'energy_cost': month_energy_cost,
        'avg_price': month_energy_cost / month_energy_mwh if month_energy_mwh > 0 else 0
    }

print("=" * 80)
print("PART 1: ANNUAL ENERGY COST")
print("=" * 80)
print(f"Total Annual Energy Consumption: {total_energy_mwh:,.0f} MWh")
print(f"Total Annual Energy Cost: ${total_energy_cost:,.2f}")
print(f"Average Energy Price: ${total_energy_cost/total_energy_mwh:.2f}/MWh")
print()

# Monthly breakdown
print(f"{'Month':<8} {'Season':<8} {'Energy (MWh)':>15} {'Energy Cost ($)':>18} {'Avg Price ($/MWh)':>18}")
print("-" * 70)
for month in range(1, 13):
    mb = monthly_breakdown[month]
    print(f"{month:<8} {mb['season']:<8} {mb['energy_mwh']:>15,.0f} {mb['energy_cost']:>18,.0f} {mb['avg_price']:>18,.2f}")

print(f"\n{'TOTAL':<16} {total_energy_mwh:>15,.0f} {total_energy_cost:>18,.0f}")

# For the data center, the load at each 4CP interval is determined by the 
# summer profile at the corresponding hour.
# 
# The 4CP intervals all occurred at hour ending 17 or 18 (i.e., 16:00-17:00 or 17:00-18:00)
# In the summer profile:
#   Hour 16 (index 16, hour ending 17): 1.00
#   Hour 17 (index 17, hour ending 18): 0.97
#
# Wait - need to be careful about hour indexing.
# The profile arrays are indexed 0-23, where index 0 = hour 0 (midnight to 1am)
# Index 16 = hour 16 (4pm to 5pm) = hour ending 17
# Index 17 = hour 17 (5pm to 6pm) = hour ending 18
#
# 4CP intervals:
# June 19, 17:00 -> hour ending 17 -> hour 16 in 0-indexed -> profile[16] = 1.00
# July 30, 17:00 -> hour ending 17 -> hour 16 -> profile[16] = 1.00
# August 18, 17:00 -> hour ending 17 -> hour 16 -> profile[16] = 1.00
# September 4, 17:30 -> this falls in the 17:00-17:15 or 17:15-17:30 15-min interval
#   The hour ending 18 would be 17:00-18:00, but 17:30 specifically means
#   the 15-minute interval from 17:15 to 17:30, which is within hour 17 (5pm-6pm)
#   -> profile[17] = 0.97
#
# Actually, the 4CP interval time "17:30" means the interval ENDING at 17:30,
# which is 17:15-17:30. This falls in hour 17 (0-indexed), which is hour ending 18.
# profile[17] = 0.97

# 4CP loads (as fraction of 200 MW capacity)
cp_loads = {
    'June (6/19 17:00)': summer_profile[16],    # 1.00
    'July (7/30 17:00)': summer_profile[16],     # 1.00
    'August (8/18 17:00)': summer_profile[16],   # 1.00
    'September (9/4 17:30)': summer_profile[17], # 0.97
}

print("=" * 80)
print("PART 2: TRANSMISSION COST (4CP-based)")
print("=" * 80)
print()
print(f"ERCOT 2025 Transmission Rate: ${transmission_rate_per_kw_year}/kW-year")
print(f"                              = ${transmission_rate_per_mw_year:,.0f}/MW-year")
print()
print("2025 4CP Intervals (Source: Energy Edge Consulting / ERCOT):")
print(f"{'Interval':<30} {'Profile Factor':>15} {'Load (MW)':>10}")
print("-" * 55)

cp_load_values = []
for interval, factor in cp_loads.items():
    load_mw = factor * capacity_mw
    cp_load_values.append(load_mw)
    print(f"{interval:<30} {factor:>15.2f} {load_mw:>10.1f}")

avg_4cp_load = np.mean(cp_load_values)
print(f"\n{'Average 4CP Load':<30} {'':>15} {avg_4cp_load:>10.1f} MW")

# Annual transmission charge = avg_4cp_load (MW) * transmission_rate ($/MW-year)
annual_transmission_cost = avg_4cp_load * transmission_rate_per_mw_year
print(f"\nAnnual Transmission Charge = {avg_4cp_load:.1f} MW × ${transmission_rate_per_mw_year:,.0f}/MW-year")
print(f"                          = ${annual_transmission_cost:,.2f}")

# ============================================================
# PART 3: TOTAL ANNUAL ELECTRICITY COST
# ============================================================
print()
print("=" * 80)
print("PART 3: TOTAL ANNUAL ELECTRICITY COST")
print("=" * 80)
print()
print(f"  Energy Cost (Day-Ahead Houston Hub):     ${total_energy_cost:>15,.2f}")
print(f"  Transmission Cost (4CP-based):           ${annual_transmission_cost:>15,.2f}")
print(f"  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────")
print(f"  TOTAL ANNUAL ELECTRICITY COST:           ${total_energy_cost + annual_transmission_cost:>15,.2f}")
print()
print(f"  Total Energy Consumption:                {total_energy_mwh:>15,.0f} MWh")
print(f"  Average 4CP Demand:                      {avg_4cp_load:>15.1f} MW")
print(f"  Effective All-In Cost:                   ${(total_energy_cost + annual_transmission_cost)/total_energy_mwh:>15.2f} /MWh")

# Total Annual Electricity Cost for a 200 MW Data Center in Houston, TX (2025)

## Executive Summary

| Component | Annual Cost |
|---|---|
| **Energy Cost** (Day-Ahead Houston Hub LMPs) | **$48,614,451** |
| **Transmission Cost** (4CP-based) | **$13,151,720** |
| **TOTAL ANNUAL ELECTRICITY COST** | **≈ $61,766,171** |
| Total Energy Consumption | 1,384,334 MWh |
| Effective All-In Cost | ~$44.62/MWh |

---

## Part 1: Energy Cost — $48,614,451

### Methodology
I retrieved all 8,760 hourly day-ahead Houston Hub LMPs for 2025 from the ERCOT database (`ercot_db.ercot_lmp` table, `zone_name = 'houston'`, `zone_type = 'hub'`, `is_day_ahead = true`). For each month, I computed the average price for each hour-of-day, then applied the seasonal load profiles to the 200 MW capacity.

### Seasonal Mapping
- **Winter** (Dec, Jan, Feb): Winter profile
- **Spring** (Mar, Apr, May): Spring profile
- **Summer** (Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep): Summer profile
- **Fall** (Oct, Nov): Fall profile

### Monthly Breakdown

| Month | Season | Energy (MWh) | Energy Cost ($) | Avg Price ($/MWh) |
|-------|--------|-------------|----------------|-------------------|
| Jan | Winter | 118,544 | 3,460,546 | 29.19 |
| Feb | Winter | 107,072 | 3,332,667 | 31.13 |
| Mar | Spring | 117,366 | 4,009,662 | 34.16 |
| Apr | Spring | 113,580 | 4,193,615 | 36.92 |
| May | Spring | 117,366 | 4,909,319 | 41.83 |
| Jun | Summer | 113,340 | 4,085,707 | 36.05 |
| Jul | Summer | 117,118 | 4,464,319 | 38.12 |
| Aug | Summer | 117,118 | 4,829,809 | 41.24 |
| Sep | Summer | 113,340 | 3,654,162 | 32.24 |
| Oct | Fall | 117,366 | 4,037,897 | 34.40 |
| Nov | Fall | 113,580 | 4,082,604 | 35.94 |
| Dec | Winter | 118,544 | 3,554,145 | 29.98 |
| **Total** | | **1,384,334** | **$48,614,451** | **$35.12** |

**Source:** ERCOT day-ahead Houston Hub LMP data from the `ercot_db` database (8,760 hourly records for Jan 1 – Dec 31, 2025).

---

## Part 2: Transmission Cost — $13,151,720

### Applicable Transmission Rate
The ERCOT-wide wholesale transmission rate for 2025 is **$66.76/kW-year** ($66,760/MW-year), as established in **PUCT Docket No. 57491** (Commission Staff's Final Transmission Charge Matrix, March 20, 2025). This rate represents the total ERCOT Transmission Cost of Service (TCOS) revenue requirement of ~$5.4 billion divided by the ERCOT-wide 4CP demand.

**Source:** NRG Energy remarks by Travis Kavulla (Vice President, Regulatory Affairs) at the ERCOT Market Summit, Austin, TX, February 25, 2025 — *"ERCOT's transmission rate is currently $66.76 per kilowatt-year."* ([NRG Energy PDF](https://www.nrg.com/assets/documents/energy-policy/ercot-transmission-costs-and-rate-design-remarks-on-feb-25-2025.pdf))

### 4CP Calculation

Under ERCOT's 4CP rules (16 TAC § 25.192), transmission charges for large transmission-connected customers are based on their average demand during the four ERCOT coincident peak intervals — one each in June, July, August, and September.

The **official 2025 4CP intervals** (published by ERCOT on December 1, 2025) are:

| Month | 4CP Interval | ERCOT System Peak |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| June | 6/19/2025 17:00 | 77.49 GW |
| July | 7/30/2025 17:00 | 81.91 GW |
| August | 8/18/2025 17:00 | 83.97 GW |
| September | 9/04/2025 17:30 | 80.08 GW |

**Source:** [Energy Edge Consulting — "ERCOT Official 2025 4CP Intervals Released"](https://energyedge.com/ercot-official-2025-4cp-intervals-released/)

### Data Center Load at 4CP Intervals

All four 4CP intervals occurred in the late afternoon (17:00–17:30), which maps to the summer profile at hours 16–17 (0-indexed):

| 4CP Interval | Summer Profile Factor | Load (MW) |
|---|---|---|
| June (HE 17) | 0.99 | 198.0 |
| July (HE 17) | 0.99 | 198.0 |
| August (HE 17) | 0.99 | 198.0 |
| September (HE 17.5) | 0.97 | 194.0 |
| **Average 4CP Load** | | **197.0 MW** |

### Transmission Charge Calculation

$$\text{Annual Transmission Charge} = \text{Avg 4CP Load} \times \text{Transmission Rate}$$
$$= 197.0 \text{ MW} \times \$66{,}760/\text{MW-year}$$
$$= \$13{,}151{,}720$$

---

## Part 3: Total Annual Electricity Cost

| Cost Component | Amount |
|---|---|
| Energy Cost (Day-Ahead Houston Hub) | $48,614,451 |
| Transmission Cost (4CP-based, $66.76/kW-yr) | $13,151,720 |
| **TOTAL** | **≈ $61,766,171** |

### Key Metrics
- **Total annual energy consumption:** 1,384,334 MWh (~79.2% capacity factor)
- **Average 4CP demand:** 197.0 MW (98.5% of nameplate)
- **Average energy price:** $35.12/MWh
- **Effective all-in cost:** ~$44.62/MWh

### Assumptions
1. The data center is exposed to day-ahead ERCOT Houston Hub prices for all energy consumption (no bilateral PPAs or real-time exposure).
2. The ERCOT-wide transmission rate of $66.76/kW-year applies as the applicable transmission rate for 2025 (per PUCT Docket 57491). This is the system-wide rate; individual TSP rates (e.g., CenterPoint Houston's specific TCOS rate) may differ slightly, but the $66.76/kW-year is the commonly cited ERCOT-wide figure used for large load cost analysis.
3. The data center does not curtail during 4CP intervals (runs at full profile throughout).
4. Only energy and transmission-related charges are included — no distribution charges, ancillary service charges, metering fees, or taxes are considered, per the question's scope.
5. The seasonal profiles apply uniformly across all days within each season, and the average monthly-hourly day-ahead prices represent the expected energy costs.
